explanation.rule: The suffix -ness forms abstract nouns from adjectives, expressing a quality, state, or condition. It is one of the most productive suffixes in English. Spelling adjustments may apply: adjectives ending in -y change y to i (lonely → loneliness); some short adjectives double the final consonant (sad → sadness).

groups.note: Attaches directly to most adjectives. Key spelling rules: -y → -i before -ness (lonely → loneliness); some short adjectives double the final consonant (sad → sadness). The resulting noun expresses a state or quality.

examples.definition: knowledge or perception of a situation or fact
the state of being isolated or without company
the degree to which something produces a desired result
the quality of being primarily concerned with one's own interests
the quality of being ready and eager to do something

wordForms.definition: knowledge or perception of a fact, situation, or the wider world
wordForms.example: The campaign aimed to raise awareness of the dangers of plastic pollution.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having knowledge or perception of a situation or fact

wordForms.definition: the state of being awake and aware of one's surroundings and thoughts
wordForms.example: The philosopher argued that consciousness cannot be fully explained by neuroscience alone.
wordForms.rootDefinition: aware of and responding to one's surroundings; awake and thinking

wordForms.definition: the degree to which something successfully produces a desired or intended result
wordForms.example: Researchers have questioned the effectiveness of the new treatment protocol.
wordForms.rootDefinition: producing the intended or expected result

wordForms.definition: the quality of treating people equally and according to their needs without bias
wordForms.example: The trade union called for greater fairness in the distribution of bonuses.
wordForms.rootDefinition: treating people equally; in accordance with rules or standards

wordForms.definition: the quality of being generous, caring, and considerate towards others
wordForms.example: She was deeply moved by the unexpected kindness of strangers.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having or showing a gentle, caring, and helpful nature

wordForms.definition: the state of being isolated and lacking companionship or social connection
wordForms.example: Chronic loneliness is increasingly recognised as a serious public health concern.
wordForms.rootDefinition: feeling sad because one has no friends or company; isolated

wordForms.definition: the state or feeling of sorrow, unhappiness, or grief
wordForms.example: There was a profound sadness in his voice as he spoke about his lost years.

wordForms.definition: the quality of being concerned primarily with one's own interests, often at others' expense
wordForms.example: His colleagues were frustrated by what they saw as selfishness in the negotiations.
wordForms.rootDefinition: lacking consideration for others; concerned chiefly with one's own benefit

wordForms.definition: the state of lacking physical or moral strength, or a particular fault or failing
wordForms.example: Admitting a weakness can actually be a sign of self-awareness and strength.

wordForms.definition: the quality of being ready and prepared to do something, often without being asked
wordForms.example: Her willingness to take on extra responsibilities impressed the entire management team.
wordForms.rootDefinition: ready, eager, or prepared to do something

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: The charity's work has significantly increased public ___ of food poverty.
The committee praised his ___ to compromise under such difficult circumstances.
The study evaluated the ___ of mindfulness programmes in reducing workplace stress.
The report highlighted ___ as a growing epidemic among urban professionals.
Employees cited a lack of ___ in performance reviews as their main complaint.

transformExercises.sentence: The campaign focused on raising ___ of mental health issues among young people. (AWARE)
The philosopher devoted her career to the study of human ___. (CONSCIOUS)
Despite his great ___, he found the courage to ask for help. (WEAK)
Her ___ in sharing credit with her team earned her great respect. (KIND)
The negotiator's ___ to make concessions eventually broke the deadlock. (WILLING)
